questions.question: Why can naive retries make an outage worse?
options: Retries use more disk space
When many clients retry a struggling dependency at once, they multiply its load, causing more failures and more retries — a retry storm
Retries always corrupt data
Retries are slower than giving up
questions.explanation: A struggling dependency that times out gets immediately retried by every caller, doubling its load right when it's overwhelmed. More timeouts trigger more retries, spiraling a recoverable slowdown into a full collapse — the retries become the attack.

questions.question: Why add jitter (randomness) to exponential backoff?
options: To make the code harder to read
To de-synchronize retries so clients that failed together don't all retry at the same backed-off moment, re-creating a spike
Jitter makes retries faster
It encrypts the retry
questions.explanation: Exponential backoff alone still has all clients retry at the same later time if they failed together — just a delayed synchronized spike. Jitter randomizes each client's wait so retries spread out, actually breaking the thundering herd.

questions.question: What does a circuit breaker do in its 'Open' state?
options: Retries the dependency as fast as possible
Rejects calls immediately (fails fast) without calling the dependency, protecting the caller from wasted timeouts
Sends all traffic to a backup database
Logs errors but still calls the dependency
questions.explanation: When Open (tripped), the breaker fails fast — returning an error instantly instead of calling the known-bad dependency. This stops callers from wasting seconds on timeouts and exhausting their own resources, and stops piling load on the dead dependency.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the circuit breaker's 'Half-Open' state?
options: To permanently block the dependency
To let a single trial call through after a cooldown to test whether the dependency has recovered, returning to Closed if it succeeds
To double the request rate
To skip the dependency forever
questions.explanation: After a cooldown, the breaker moves to Half-Open and allows one trial call. Success means the dependency recovered → back to Closed (normal). Failure → back to Open for another cooldown. This probes for recovery without flooding a still-broken dependency.

questions.question: When is retrying a failed call NOT safe?
options: Never — retries are always safe
For non-idempotent operations (e.g. a payment) or deterministic failures, where retrying causes double effects or just wastes resources
Only on weekends
Only for read operations
questions.explanation: Retries suit transient failures of idempotent operations. Retrying a non-idempotent payment can double-charge; retrying a deterministic failure (bad input, a real bug) will fail again and waste resources. Make operations idempotent (Phase 6) before retrying side effects.
